name: Colada
description: Colada is one of the two swords attributed to El Cid Campeador, the legendary Castilian nobleman and military leader of medieval Spain. The sword is said to possess immense power and prestige, representing El Cid's valor, honor, and military prowess. According to the epic poem, 'Cantar de Mio Cid,' Colada, along with Tizona, were won by El Cid in battle against the Moorish king Bucar of Valencia. Colada is described as being exceptionally sharp and capable of inspiring fear and respect in El Cid's enemies. Legend claims that the sword's mere presence on the battlefield could demoralize opposing forces. The sword is also associated with stories of supernatural abilities, such as being able to detect cowardice in its wielder. While the historical accuracy of the sword's existence and powers remains debated, Colada holds significant cultural importance in Spain, symbolizing the spirit of the Reconquista and the enduring legacy of El Cid. It represents the ideals of chivalry, courage, and justice, making it a powerful symbol in Spanish national identity. The sword is often depicted in art and literature, further solidifying its place in Spanish folklore and collective memory. Though its current whereabouts are unknown, the legend of Colada continues to inspire and captivate, reminding us of the heroic tales of El Cid and the rich history of medieval Spain.
subtitle: El Cid's legendary sword of valor
origin: Won in battle against King Bucar of Valencia

keyMyths.title: Victory over Bucar
keyMyths.description: El Cid wins Colada after defeating King Bucar of Valencia in battle. The sword becomes a symbol of his victory and military skill.

keyMyths.title: Detection of Cowardice
keyMyths.description: Legend says Colada can detect cowardice in its wielder, shining less brightly or even breaking if held by someone lacking courage. This reinforces El Cid's reputation for bravery.

sources.author: Anonymous
